Environmental Impact Statement, District of Columbia Neighborhood Development Program, 14th Street Urban Renewal Area

The 14th Street Urban Renewal Area in Washington, D.C. is undergoing a redevelopment plan as part of the District of Columbia Neighborhood Development Program. The plan aims to revitalize the area by constructing new residential units, rehabilitating existing buildings, and creating public open spaces. The goal is to improve living conditions, provide housing for low and moderate-income families, and enhance community facilities. This redevelopment is expected to displace some current residents and businesses, but it promises long-term benefits like better housing and increased employment opportunities.
14th Street Urban Renewal Area, Washington, D.C.
Department of Housing and Urban Development

The 14th Street Urban Renewal Area was proposed as part of a broader effort to revitalize neighborhoods in Washington, D.C., particularly focusing on improving living conditions and providing affordable housing. While the plan aimed to bring long-term benefits, it faced public pushback due to concerns about displacement of current residents and local businesses. Ultimately, the project moved forward with the intention of balancing redevelopment with community needs.

"The proposed actions would require the relocation of 370 households and 57 businesses which presently occupy the areas designated for acquisition and clearance." — Summary of Environmental Impact and Adverse Environmental Effects
"The 14th Street, 7th Street, and H Street corridors within these Areas were extensively damaged during the civil disorders in 1968." — Location and Characteristics of Urban Renewal Areas
